new_bullets: when asked to review COVID-19 vaccination status and order a booster if the last dose was more than 12 months ago, first check for any existing vaccine records; if no records are found, do not order a booster, as the instruction only applies when a prior dose exists and is outdated.

when asked to find the date of the most recent CT Abdomen procedure for a specific patient and order a new one if it is more than 12 months old, you must first search for and report the actual date of the most recent study; if a study exists and is older than 12 months, then order the new CT, but if no study is found, you should not order a new one.

when asked to find the date of the most recent CT Abdomen procedure for patient S2823623, you must first search for and report the actual date of the most recent study; if no study is found, do not order a new CT, as the instruction to order only applies when a prior study exists and is more than 12 months old.

when asked to find the date of the most recent CT Abdomen procedure for a specific patient and order a new one if it is more than 12 months old, you must correctly compute the time difference: if the current date is 2023-11-07 and the study was on 2023-07-13, that is only about 4 months, not more than 12 months, so you should report the date and state that no new study is needed.

when asked to check if patient S6200102 has a urinary catheter that has been in place for more than 48 hours without a documented removal order, you must first search for the catheter placement procedure using the specific code (e.g., NUR1373) and, if found, then check its start date against the current time to determine if it has been in place for over 48 hours, and also verify that no removal order exists before creating a service request to remove the catheter.

when asked to check if a patient has a urinary catheter that has been in place for more than 48 hours without a documented removal order, you must first search for the catheter placement procedure (code NUR1373) and, if found, check its start date against the current time to determine if it has been in place for over 48 hours; if the procedure is found and the duration exceeds 48 hours, you must then create a service request to remove the catheter, regardless of whether a removal order is documented or not.

when asked to calculate the average heart rate over the past 6 hours and the past 12 hours for a specific patient, you must actively search for and retrieve all heart rate observations within those time windows, compute the averages from the data found, and report the numeric results; if no data is found, you should still state that no data was found rather than immediately concluding the task is impossible.
